data.title: Compounding, Leverage, And Discipline: The 25-Year Path From $250K To $10M
data.description:
Investing in an underappreciated AI winner, clean energy disruptor, or under-the-radar biotech player: what is the best way to turn a $250,000 investment into a potentially life-changing $10 million over the next quarter-century?

In this article, I will argue that the answer is likely "none of the above". I will then explain how one can position their portfolio to reasonably hope (since "expect" might imply overconfidence) to achieve the nearly 16% annual returns needed to multiply one's nest egg by 40x over the lifespan of a young adult.

I'm not great at picking winners — and likely neither are you

As the chart below suggests, an investment in Microsoft (MSFT) 25 years ago would have made a lot more sense than one in Macy's (M). If I could travel back to 2001 and tell my younger self to pick one of these two stocks, the answer would have been clear. The problem is that my DeLorean prototype has shown no signs of being functional.

As ludicrous as the idea of hindsight investing might be, I believe this is how many investors manage their portfolios: they try to beat the market at identifying the next hot stock or group of them that will, like MSFT over the years, deliver something like mid-teen annual gains in the long run.

It is worth noting that, according to research by Hendrik Bessembinder (Arizona State), the median stock underperforms T-bills. As many as 65% of US stocks failed to produce the average 9% to 10% annual returns over a multi-decade period since the 1920s, with a large fraction of them delivering low single-digit or negative returns. Finding the next group of Microsoft stocks, therefore, is incredibly difficult.

I have a different approach, one that starts with a very simple assumption: I am not good at reliably and consistently predicting the future; therefore, I should not count on being successful at it. If this is the case, then my best hope at making money in the markets is not by picking winners based on my convictions but by "riding beta" higher over time.

Take bonds, for example. The 10-year US Treasury yield is about 4.1% today, which is the expected rate of return of an investment in these instruments when held to maturity and assuming no default. Also think about stocks, except that the cash flow production and distribution to investors are less certain (that is, higher risk and a higher discount rate). Still, as a large group and over the long haul, stocks are very likely to produce positive returns. For instance, the S&P 500 (SPY) has returned a minimum annualized gain of about 1% over any rolling 25-year period (and about 10% on average). This particular worst-case scenario surprisingly included the dreaded 1930s Great Depression in its entirety.

Riding beta, therefore, means investing in assets that are very likely to appreciate over time due to a fundamental reason, usually future cash flow production valued at what they are worth today (although exceptions probably exist in gold and other traditional stores of value). Riding beta also means that there is little to no value in playing the resource-intensive alpha game — i.e., deploying time, money, and sanity in trying to outsmart the market.

Establishing the compounding framework

Disclaimer: this section is a thought experiment and does not perfectly mirror (far from it) what we observe in the real world, yet I encourage the reader to hang in there.

The chart below is a back-of-the-envelope, gross representation of what riding beta over a 25-year period might look like: a steady climb over the long haul, punctuated by ebbs and flows that are representative of market cycles. Don't worry, for now, that this portfolio grows very predictably and noise-free, as we will address this topic in the next section.

In a perfect world in which I have high confidence that my investments will grow over time following the exact pattern above, I can introduce a trading strategy that would vastly improve the portfolio's absolute and risk-adjusted performance: cut the losses on the way down and only ride the upside. More specifically, I could choose to own the assets in the portfolio when the four-week (roughly 22-day) moving average is above the forty-week (roughly 200-day) moving average, thus protecting my downside while exposing me to both (1) rebounds from cycle bottoms and (2) the general move higher over the decades.

Below is how the "cut the losses" approach would perform vs. buy-and-hold, followed by a graphical representation:

  • Annual return of 20.6% vs. 5.5%
  • Annualized volatility of 9.0% vs. 12.8%
  • Sharpe ratio (assuming a zero risk-free rate) of 2.29 vs. 0.43
  • Maximum drawdown of -16.9% vs. 41.8%

The results are so much better because the portfolio's value never declines substantially. The recovery, when it happens, starts from a higher plateau, and the cycle repeats over and over.

Enter diversification

To reiterate, the above is a thought exercise that is unlikely to be seen in the real world, since asset prices do not follow a clean, noise-free path through economic and shorter-term market cycles. Life is much, much messier than this.

For instance, the chart below shows the performance of the S&P 500 since the 1990s. Notice that:

  • The general trend is still up and to the right
  • However, the ebbs and flows are much more irregular: they are often barely noticeable on the chart, but sometimes quite evident and disruptive (e.g., early 2000s, Great Financial Crisis of 2008, COVID-19 outbreak, and the 2022 inflation shock)

Despite the irregular patterns in stock prices seen historically, something can be done to smooth out the imperfections, to a large extent: multi-asset class diversification.

Using a framework developed by hedge fund Bridgewater Associates (see table below), asset prices react to the deviation in growth and inflation vs. market expectations. For example, equities tend to rise when economic growth rises and also when inflation falls. Inversely, inflation-linked government bonds tend to fall in these same scenarios. Therefore, in order to smooth out the return stream of a portfolio, an investor might choose a risk-balanced blend of stocks and IL bonds (i.e., TIPS) and/or commodities and nominal bonds.

To illustrate the idea, below is a very simple, three-asset portfolio composed of 1/3 SPY, 1/3 long-term treasuries (TLT), and 1/3 gold (GLD) since the mid-2000s, rebalanced daily to simplify the calculations, compared to the performance of the S&P 500 alone.

Notice how the blue line is much smoother and more closely resembles (even if it does not perfectly mimic) the pie-in-the-sky portfolio depicted earlier: it climbs higher over time, but also with slightly more predictable and less erratic ebbs and flows.

The more predictable return stream means that a diversified portfolio is a better candidate for our "ride the beta, limit large losses" investment approach. I ran the stock-bond-gold portfolio through the trading strategy of following the moving averages as I described before, and the results were quite solid.

It is true that this proposed "balanced plus risk-capped" portfolio would have returned slightly lower-than-market absolute returns, in great part due to the mediocre (not to say awful) performance of treasuries and other investment-grade bonds since the second half of 2020. However, the risk-adjusted performance would have been better than that of the high-flying S&P 500 (Sharpe ratio of 1.03 vs. 0.99); volatility would have been nearly one-fifth lower; and the maximum drawdown would have been nearly one-third less damaging.

To be clear, I have only considered, so far, a simple portfolio of mega-cap US stocks, long-duration treasuries, and gold. An even better portfolio might include other securities, such as inflation-protected assets, non-US public equity and debt, and maybe even cryptocurrencies. For example, compared to the three assets originally proposed, the following allocation would have likely served as a better base portfolio for our "ride the beta, limit large losses" strategy, having produced a Sharpe ratio of 1.62 since 2019:

  • 26% Nasdaq 100 (QQQ)
  • 33% gold
  • 39% managed futures (DBMF)
  • 2% diversified commodities (DBC)

Enter leverage

So far, we have talked about the compounding framework and the need to first create an efficient portfolio that produces as much return as possible per unit of risk. But an elegant, high-Sharpe portfolio might not necessarily be expected to produce the nearly 16% annual returns needed to turn $250,000 into $10 million in 25 years, as the title of the article suggests. For that, leverage is likely needed.

When it comes to leverage, I should warn readers that using it is not for everyone. If not deployed and monitored properly (think of our moving average approach to risk management), even when applied to a risk-balanced and diversified portfolio, leverage can cause significant damage. As a classic example, a boring 50/50 blend of US stocks and long-duration bonds that was levered by a factor of two would have lost 51% in 2022 if left unmanaged.

Leverage can be produced in a few different ways. Borrowing from one's broker is one route that investors registered to do so might consider. Currently, Interactive Brokers lends at one percentage point above a benchmark rate of 3.6% for amounts ranging from $100,000 to $1 million, which today represents about 10 bps below the 30-year treasury yield.

My preferred method, however, is through the use of derivatives. While deep in-the-money call options can do the trick, going long on a futures contract that tracks the desired underlying asset is arguably the simplest way to leverage a position with minimal margin requirements (i.e., usually around 6% of the notional only for the S&P 500).

Backtesting the strategy

Let's go back to the Bridgewater table above and put all the pieces of the puzzle together. Assume I picked one or two key assets from each of the four quadrants, balanced them roughly in accordance with their volatility (e.g., an asset that is half as volatile as its peer gets twice the allocation), leveraged the portfolio at 2.5x, and managed the drawdowns using the moving average approach. The (1) portfolio allocation and (2) performance over the past 15 years would have been as follows:

  • 50% Nasdaq 100: rising growth
  • 75% long-duration TIPS (LTPZ): falling growth, but also rising inflation
  • 50% gold: rising inflation
  • 30% global stocks (VT) 40% investment-grade LT corporate bonds: falling inflation

  • Annualized returns of 19.4%
  • Annualized volatility of 20.9%
  • Maximum drawdown of -39.7%

Notice how the portfolio proposed above would have performed in line with the almighty Nasdaq 100 (slightly better absolute return, slightly worse risk metrics) over the past decade and a half, which included a major recession recovery, European debt crisis, political shakeups, COVID-19 pandemic and rebound, and inflation shock.

Worth noting, the portfolio would have achieved the feat while allocating 115% to long-duration bonds (most of which are inflation-linked) that have performed terribly for years (see below). Also note that volatility and drawdowns in this portfolio would have been only minimally worse than those of the Nasdaq 100, despite the sizable 2.5x leverage factor.

Looking forward

As we all know, past performance does not predict future results. If I were to build a portfolio of $250,000 today, looking to turn it into $10 million in 25 years using the framework above, I would make a few key adjustments to the proposed allocation.

While the Nasdaq 100 has been an outstanding performer since the end of the 2001-2003 dot-com crash and ensuing recession, I prefer not to bet that tech stocks will continue to dominate old-economy sectors, although they very well may. Rather than holding QQQ, I would likely look to strike a balance with the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DIA) and the Russell 2000 index of small caps (IWM) — likely still with a bias towards the technologies of the future, understanding that managing the downside helps to deal with the possibility that AI, for example, could undergo a bubble-bursting process.

I would also be cautious with my fixed-income exposure. First, considering the ballooning federal government debt in the US, I would likely lean more heavily than usual in the direction of high-quality corporate debt, at least to capture some diversification benefit. Second, non-US instruments might be a good idea to hedge against a falling dollar.

Lastly, I would probably find room for managed futures strategies in the portfolio. Funds like the Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF (CTA), KraneShares Mount Lucas Managed Futures Index Strategy ETF (KMLM), and iMGP DBi Managed Futures Strategy ETF (DBMF), blended at a ratio of one-third each, have produced annual returns of over 6% with an impressive correlation of -0.5 (negative) against the S&P 500. I believe that trend-following can vastly improve risk-adjusted performance, although leverage would be needed to turn modest returns into something that can significantly add to the target of 16%.

Make no mistake: producing such high performance is far from easy and requires a bit of everything: diligence, patience, guts (to leverage, for example), and certainly a bit of luck. However, if an investor were to take on the challenge, I believe that this would be the best way to tackle it in a way that increases his or her chances of achieving success.

data.title: The Great Rotation From Tech To REITs Is Finally Here
data.description:
I have been warning about this since last Summer:

AI is a major headwind, not a tailwind, for most stocks.

Yes, it may boost profitability in the short-run by reducing costs and increasing margins, but at the same time, it also breaks down traditional barriers to entry and erodes moats that have historically protected the long-term earnings power of businesses from future potential competitors.

Anything from...

  • Designing a product
  • Setting up a website
  • Writing a landing page
  • Creating ads
  • Handling your accounting
  • Drafting contracts
  • Customer service
  • Logistics
  • And much more.

... can now be handled by AI almost instantaneously and at no cost.

Put simply, it is becoming easier than ever before to start almost any type of business, and this is happening right at a time when a lot of ambitious and smart people will lose their jobs and struggle to find a new one.

I think that the result will be an explosion in new small business formations across most fields, hurting the profitability of established players.

Some businesses will, of course, be more affected than others, and arguably the worst-positioned are digital businesses like SaaS companies (Software as a Service) because a solo-entrepreneur with AI can now replicate their entire business models with very low resources.

The market has now finally begun to recognize this, and it has caused investors to run away from software companies.

The change in market sentiment has been truly spectacular. They went from being perceived as capital-light companies with growing recurring revenue streams benefiting from the AI tailwind to now being perceived as easy-to-replicate businesses with no barriers to entry that are waiting to be disrupted by cheaper AI-powered start-ups.

Here Are the Results of This Capital Run:

The large, well-established software companies like Intuit (INTU) (QuickBooks, TurboTax, MailChimp, etc.), Constellation Software (CNSWF), and Roper Technologies (ROP) have had their share prices cut in half in a matter of months:

But it is not just software companies.

Lots of other tech companies are also taking it on the chin as AI breaks traditional moats and the market predicts that new competitors will soon emerge.

Even Microsoft (MSFT), Salesforce (CRM), and Workday (WDAY) are down big, and now trade at near their lowest valuation in the last 5 years:

Fintech companies have also been hit particularly hard. PayPal (PYPL), Robinhood (HOOD), and Coinbase (COIN) are down very big:

Now, I recognize that AI is not the only reason why these stocks have sold off, but it is one major contributor.

These companies portray themselves as tech companies, and the valuable tech that they have developed can now potentially be replicated at a tiny fraction of the cost by new AI-native start-ups.

EdTech and HealthTech stocks are also suffering a lot. Once popular stocks like Duolingo (DUOL) and Hims (HIMS) have lost most of their value over the past year, and the sell-off clearly accelerated as a result of these new AI fears:

But guess which sector didn't suffer at all from this recent selloff, and on the contrary, even benefited from new capital flows that pushed their share prices higher?

The answer is REITs (VNQ).

REITs are the ultimate anti-AI asset class because they are the polar opposite of anything digital and cannot be replicated by AI.

They own real assets that are essential to our society and strictly limited in their supply by their land, building permits, financing, and construction costs.

Therefore, it makes sense that REITs would become a safe haven as investors become concerned about how AI will disrupt digital businesses.

It does not get more "real" than having a roof over your head. That need will never go away, and AI will never be able to replicate an apartment community at a low cost in a popular location of a growing market that's already built out.

The capital rotation appears to have now begun, but I think that this is still the beginning.

The market has just now recognized that AI is likely to disrupt a lot of tech companies, but it still misses that this disruption is likely to spread far beyond that over time.

I think that it will hurt most companies across most sectors by lowering barriers to entry, making it easier to start new businesses, inviting new competitors, and lowering profits for each individual company.

I have run various businesses myself for close to a decade, and the main thing that makes it difficult to start a business is that you must know how to handle so many different tasks all at once. Each individual task may be fairly easy and cheap to handle, but put together, it is a lot and can be very complex and expensive.

That has historically been the biggest barrier for most people to get started.

But not anymore with AI supporting you through all the different tasks.

Even businesses that you may think are safe today probably won't be for very long. Put simply, businesses are being commoditized, and former competitive advantages like expertise and scale are rapidly losing their value.

My prediction is that as more and more investors come to this conclusion, the market will become increasingly worried about the future, and more capital will shift to those anti-AI asset classes that won't be impacted.

In a world in which AI is disrupting most equities, wealth preservation will become the primary concern of investors, leading to a lot greater allocation towards assets like REITs, and this could lead to significant upside given how cheap REITs are today.

They are, in fact, still trading at their lowest valuations since the Great Financial Crisis relative to equities:

And lots of individual REITs are today trading at large discounts relative to the private market value of their properties.

This explains why REITs like Camden Property Trust (CPT) are aggressively buying back shares and why private equity players like Blackstone (BX) and Brookfield (BAM) keep acquiring REITs left and right.

The latest REIT buyout happened just last week, as Brookfield agreed to acquire Peakstone Realty Trust (PKST) at a 32% premium.

Brookfield is one of the most sophisticated private equity investors in the world, and they think that they are getting a good deal even after paying such a steep premium.

That's how cheap REITs currently are.

But potentially not for much longer, as increasingly many investors turn to anti-AI asset classes to protect their portfolios, even if that's just for the sake of diversification.

Investor Takeaway

I think that the long-term impact of AI on businesses is still widely underestimated by the market.

I predicted this recent SaaS disruption already back in the Summer of 2025. I now predict that this is still just the beginning, with many more sectors of the market likely being affected over the long run.

That is why it is so critical for investors to allocate more heavily into anti-AI asset classes like REITs today to not only protect their portfolio but also to enjoy upside as more investors come to this same conclusion and bid up their share prices to higher levels.

At High Yield Landlord, we have positioned our portfolio to maximize gains from this capital rotation that's waiting to happen.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Telekomunikasi Indonesia: Indonesia's Digital Backbone At An Attractive Discount And Solid Yield
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Telekomunikasi Indonesia (TLK), commonly known as Telkom Indonesia, is the country's largest telecom company, currently undergoing a strategic pivot and trading at an attractive valuation, standing to benefit from several long-term tailwinds, including a potential rotation, more attention for Indonesia given their strong growth outlook, and even data center demand.

The company’s free cash flow reached IDR31,260 billion during the first 9 months of 2025, meaning roughly $1.86 billion, backed by a solid decrease in CAPEX as the company is adapting and progressing on their value-unlocking agenda, including the spin-off of their fiber assets into PT Telkom Infrastruktur Indonesia.

As for the guidance, the company reduced their revenue expectations to a slight contraction, while the EBITDA margin would remain at ~50% and the CAPEX-to-revenue ratio at ~17% to 19%, meaning there should be a spike in Q4 CAPEX, which isn’t uncommon for them and the industry.

As we can see, Q4 was bigger for them in terms of CAPEX (nearly IDR30 trillion in FY24 compared to a bit over IDR21 trillion in 9M’24), although normalizing a bit compared to prior years.

For them to reach their 2025 guidance, if we assume IDR145 trillion in revenue (slight drop from nearly IDR150 trillion in 2024), we should be looking at about IDR26.1 trillion in CAPEX based on the midpoint expectation, meaning they have to spend another roughly IDR8 trillion in Q4 2025, potentially getting us to a free cash flow of about IDR33 trillion ($1.96 billion), but I’ll round it up to $2 billion to assume that the Q4 operating cash flow is also better.

Financially, based on TLK’s latest report, we see an overall solid position, as even though their current assets are below the current liabilities (not uncommon for the industry), their debt is quite limited, which is a very big difference compared to American telecom giants like Verizon (VZ) - which, as a note, has a debt that’s about 10% of Indonesia’s GDP - or AT&T (T).

The company is expected to report earnings on March 6, and I’ll mention again that I expect a mix of higher CAPEX with slightly better OCF, as the company’s Q4 tends to be higher in both cases due to the seasonality of their operations, working capital, etc.

I don’t expect this to deliver some impressive growth organically, but I’m looking forward to any news regarding spin-offs or other strategic initiatives, as well as a guidance for 2026 and insights into the environment, as the company can be a great indicator of the economy’s “pulse” given their vital operations.

Dividends & Buybacks

The company's annual dividend reached IDR 21 trillion (~$1.25 billion today), for a yield of roughly 6% based on today's prices, which is not terrible given the fact that it represents a payout ratio of roughly 62.5% of their free cash flow based on my estimate for this year and about 65.79% based on the ~$1.90 billion FCF from 2024.

Additionally, they talk about potential value unlocking more dividends thanks to their spin-offs and the potential high-growth data center investments combined with the streamlined operations, while recently announcing an ~IDR3 trillion in buybacks from May 2025 to May 2026.

Risks & Opportunities

One opportunity we can see the company operating on is now being under Danantara (the new Sovereign Wealth Fund, founded about a year ago), which kind of changes their approach from the legacy “be everywhere, no matter if you lose money” to “be efficient and optimize,” potentially unlocking a lot of value.

In other words, we could say that they’re moving away from a “state utility” kind of investment to more of an infrastructure holding company with a higher focus on profitability.

This comes as the company is optimizing their portfolio (spin-offs mentioned above) and focusing on profitability, spinning off infrastructure (similar to towers, they're spinning off fiber optic cables and network businesses into InfraNexia), potentially unlocking more value from their legacy assets, while expanding in data centers.

However, despite their internal reshuffling that can unlock long-term, we have to look at the broader picture. Indonesian consumer spending has been soft in 2025, with studies showing that despite a solid ~5.39% YoY in Q4 2025, its foundation remains troubled, as the economy still faces several structural bottlenecks, including stagnant productivity in labor-intensive manufacturing, dependency on commodities, and a lack of integration into global value chains.

Still, that also means there is more potential to recover, while the company’s reshuffling and new focus on value and optimization could come at a great time, as the region also stands to benefit from a potential recovery in global tourism, with tourism in Asia Pacific still down ~9% from the 2019 levels, with plenty of potential to recover and grow well beyond that, which can benefit the country and TLK.

Meanwhile, Indonesia is expected to remain one of the strongest of the ASEAN-6 (i.e., Vietnam, Philippines, Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand), with the country’s GDP expected to grow at a whopping 5.6% per year from 2025 to 2040, which is a monumental improvement compared to the US/EU/others, making it one of the most attractive countries on the planet from this point of view, in my opinion.

Regarding data centers, the company’s push for an aggressive expansion could also turn out to be very smart, as the country has massive potential as a “data center hub” in the region, benefitting from relatively cheap energy, resources, workforce, etc.

Also, given the company’s size, health, and vital operations, they could stand to benefit significantly from a potential capital rotation into the region. As mentioned before, this can be a response to the market adapting to the geopolitical uncertainty, falling dollar, Japan’s economic plans potentially hurting the USD and US treasuries (as they are the #1 foreign holder), record levels of debt in the US (among others), crisis fears, and many other factors that lead to the market changing its expectations surrounding the US and potentially diversifying.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, despite being a more defensive play, the company will still depend on the environment, and potential macro pressure, especially now, can affect the efficiency of their portfolio reshuffling, as they risk selling at a discount in a soft market, while there is potential to see more weakness in the consumer environment as well.

Meanwhile, being a state-owned company (majority owned by the Danantara fund, which also pushed for higher dividends) exposes them to political risk even more, with the new fund potentially pushing them into “optimizing at any cost,” which could be just as bad and a threat for their long-term profitability.

Valuation

For TLK’s DCF to Equity valuation, I’ll start with my estimated $2 billion in FCF in 2025, assuming a 6% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to the potential coming from their strategic reshuffle and high-growth initiatives on top of the country’s potential, with a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 3.25% terminal growth rate thanks to Indonesia’s growth outlook (still well below it), with a 12.5% discount rate to take into account the country risks and competition, as well as 99.06 billion shares outstanding (a 1-for-100 ADS conversion rate) as of their latest disclosure.

This way, we get to an equity value of $25.49 billion, for an intrinsic value of $25.73 for TLK, which is above the current $20.96 at the moment of writing this despite using relatively conservative estimates.

Conclusions

TLK is a buy, as the company trades at an attractive valuation that supports a solid yield, standing to benefit from the potential of one of the best countries in terms of long-term potential through a vital service.

Despite the stock's strong performance, I believe there is still some steam left, while they still stand to benefit from macro improvements that can benefit their long-term, potentially outweighing the risks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hunting For Dividends: Is ASEAN Next?
data.description:
For years, ASEAN[1] has been framed almost exclusively as a growth story. Favourable demographics, rising consumption and the relocation of manufacturing have positioned Southeast Asia as one of the most dynamic emerging regions in the world. But as the region’s economy matures, a new narrative is taking shape. ASEAN is no longer just about growth: it is increasingly becoming a compelling destination for dividend seekers.

A region where growth meets income

ASEAN’s dividend opportunity is underpinned by diverse and evolving market characteristics. Singapore’s domestically focused economy promises strong and stable yields across banks, alongside telecoms and infrastructure-linked companies with regional exposure. Indonesia’s dividend income is rising, led by maturing banks, telecoms stocks and resource companies that are increasingly returning cash to shareholders. Malaysia contributes income through its strengths in advanced manufacturing, government-linked enterprises and Islamic finance, while Thailand’s dividend profile is supported by energy, utilities and infrastructure operators with historically steady cash flows. The Philippines rounds out the picture with emerging dividend potential, driven by banks and utilities, as its young population and expanding consumer base gradually translate growth into shareholder payouts.

The FTSE ASEAN Index, which captures the large- and mid-cap companies listed in the five ASEAN markets—Singapore, Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand and Philippines—has delivered a 10-year average dividend yield of 3.57%. This has exceeded the yields of major global benchmarks, including FTSE Asia Pacific ex Japan Australia and New Zealand Index (2.49%), FTSE USA Index (1.68%), FTSE Developed Europe Index (3.18%) and FTSE Emerging Index (2.90%).

Over the past 5 years, the FTSE ASEAN Index has recorded steady growth in cash flow per share (see the chart), reinforcing ASEAN companies’ historic ability to sustain and grow their dividends. The region’s average dividend payout ratio during this period also stands out relative to global peers, underscoring a strong commitment to providing shareholders with income. Looking ahead, ASEAN’s forward 12-month dividend yield[2] remains attractive compared with other major markets worldwide, highlighting the region’s appeal for income-oriented investors.

As of the end of 31 December 2025, more than 60% of the large- and mid-cap companies in the FTSE ASEAN Index offered dividend yields above 3%, reflecting a management culture that focuses on shareholder returns: dividends have accounted for roughly half of the FTSE ASEAN Index’s total return over time.

Dividends' contribution to the total return of the FTSE ASEAN index (2001-2025)

In an environment marked by economic uncertainty, geopolitical tensions and the prospect of declining global interest rates, investors' appetite for reliable income streams has sharpened. Against this backdrop, the ASEAN region’s combination of attractive dividend yields, relative economic resilience and long-term structural growth stands out.

A smart way to capture ASEAN income: target dividend strategy

Not all dividend strategies are created equal, however. Traditional dividend approaches often focus on the highest-yielding stocks, which can lead to excessive portfolio concentration and above-average exposure to smaller companies or to businesses with weakening fundamentals.

The FTSE ASEAN ex REITs Target Dividend Index takes a different approach. It targets a 100% yield uplift relative to the broad FTSE ASEAN Index (excluding REITs). By removing REITs, the index emphasises companies with earnings-supported dividends and lower interest rate sensitivity.

Rather than selecting the highest headline yields, the index starts by selecting companies with a consistent dividend-paying record. Using FTSE Russell’s Target Exposure methodology—a rules-based tilting framework—it gradually increases exposure to higher-yielding companies while proportionally reducing exposure to lower-yield names.

Across multiple market cycles over the past 25 years, a back-test of the FTSE ASEAN ex REITs Target Dividend Index's strategy showed notable resilience during market downturns and lower overall volatility compared with the broader ASEAN market.

Back-tested performance of the FTSE ASEAN ex REITs Target Dividend index

While the FTSE ASEAN Index (excluding REITs) delivered an average dividend yield of 3.4% over the past decade, a back-test of the FTSE ASEAN ex REITs Target Dividend Index achieved an average dividend yield of 6.5% over the same period.

The Target Dividend strategy also offers diversification benefits. Over the past 5 years, it had a low correlation with the Russell 1000 Index (0.07) and FTSE China A Index (0.20), helping to mitigate concentration risk for portfolios heavily weighted toward US or China equities.

Be mindful of yield traps

Many dividend indices simply rank stocks by yield and pick the companies at the top of the ranking. The problem with that approach is that very high yields are often a warning sign. They can come from smaller or distressed companies where the share price has already fallen sharply—creating what we call a “yield trap”. While the yield may appear attractive, it is often unsustainable as the share price fall is an advance indicator of a future dividend cut.

To mitigate this kind of risk, the FTSE ASEAN ex REITs Target Dividend Index combines momentum scoring with a robust dividend yield Z-score. The Z-score helps identify companies with abnormally high yields, while the momentum scoring helps screen out stocks with persistently weak price performance. By combining valuation discipline with a fundamentals-aware momentum check, the index avoids stocks that look attractive on yield alone but which are under genuine financial stress.

ASEAN’s next chapter

Dividend investing in the ASEAN region isn’t just a nostalgic nod to “old-school” value strategies—it’s a strategic response to today’s global economic realities, in which dividends have reclaimed their place alongside prospects for share price growth.

Long viewed purely as a growth story, ASEAN is quietly proving it can deliver something even more compelling: cash flows today with growth for tomorrow. With rising corporate payouts, innovative income-focused index solutions and a diverse regional economic base, the stage is set for dividend seekers to reassess this dynamic region. ASEAN is no longer just a growth story—it is a dividend story whose time has arrived.

data.title: S&P 500: These 2 Number Worry Me More Than High P/E
data.description:
Elevated FINRA margin ratio met with passive trading

With all equity indices (ranging from DJI, SP500, NASDAQ, and RTY) hovering around record levels, I’ve seen plenty of critics about the high P/E ratios of these indices and share my counterarguments. To be perfectly clear, I am not saying the current market is not expensive. The points I wanted to make in my counterarguments are mainly twofold. First, valuation multiples need to be contextualized (for example, an article criticizing the SP500's high P/E should also point out the SP500’s high profit margin and/or its global reach, in my view). Second, many analyses that seem/intend to run a multifaceted diagnosis on the market are still about P/E ratios in my view and thus do not add additional insights. Some of these examples I’ve read recently included the high Buffett index, the high rule of 20 scores, the high 10-year rolling returns, etc.

With the background, this article wants to address the second point. To be more explicit, I want to A) examine two specific metrics, the FINRA Margin Balance ratio and the percentage of passive trading in today’s market; B) explain why they are independent vectors from P/E ratios and thus provide additional insights; and C) explain why these 2 metrics are more worrisome in my mind than the higher P/E ratios of the SP500.

In a nutshell, I will explain why the combination of a high margin balance and a dominance of passive trading can make the stock market behave like a giant momentum trading (or voting) machine in the near term and cause large sudden volatilities. Of course, every issue provides risks and opportunities at the same time. Thus, at the end of the article, I will also brainstorm some ideas that investors can use to either hedge or capitalize on such volatilities.

FINRA margin ratio approaches historical bubble levels

The next chart shows the current and historical FINRA margin balance ratio. The data is provided by MacroMicro, and for readers unfamiliar with the ratio, the following quote should provide sufficient background for the purpose of this article:

The ratio is calculated as customers' debit balance in securities margin accounts with FINRA member firms divided by currency in circulation in the US. The ratio measures existing leverage per each dollar in circulation, which reflects capital momentum in the stock market.

With the above background, let’s examine the data more closely. As seen, the current FINRA Margin Balance ratio hovers around 0.50, almost on par with the levels preceding the dot-com bubble in the 2000s and also the 2008 Great Recession. In my mind, such an elevated margin ratio represents a "coiled spring" situation, a situation that is far more dangerous than a high P/E multiple alone. A high P/E ratio may suggest the market is overvalued, but a high margin ratio indicates that a significant portion of the market’s recent gains is funded by debt. As the ratio reaches multi-decade high levels, I see increasing odds for a relatively minor market dip to trigger margin calls on a large scale, forcing investors to sell assets regardless of their fundamentals and/or P/E ratios.

The risk is further compounded by the rise of passive trading, as detailed next.

The rise of passive trading

With the rise of index funds, passive trading has been gaining an increasing role in the equity market. According to recent data provided by DPAM, over 60% of the daily market trading volume now comes from passive trading (aka algorithmic trading) strategies.

The dominance of these automated trading algorithms is turning the S&P 500 into a giant momentum machine in my model. These strategies can—and have—overreact to short-term earnings data or even data glitches while disregarding fundamental-oriented metrics including, but not limited to, P/E ratios. Once these algorithms are triggered, they can create a feedback loop among themselves. For example, when one algorithm decides to buy stock X, it tends to increase the weight of X in the SP500 index, triggering a feedback loop for other algorithms based on the SP500 index to buy more of X too, thus further magnifying the buying pressure and price movement. The exact opposite can happen when the algorithms decide to sell.

The statistics provided in the next chart serve as a reflection of the above magnification mechanism. To wit, this chart shows the proportion of stocks moving more than 10% on the day of their earnings announcements since 2014. As seen, the overall trend is a rapid increase in volatility. In 2014, the proportion is slightly above 10%. By the end of 2024, the reading surged to 37%, i.e., more than 1 of 3 companies experienced a more than 10% price movement on the day of their earnings report. No matter how fast-paced our lives have become, I simply refuse to believe that these many companies’ fundamentals can change more than 10% from quarter to quarter.

Other risks and final thoughts

And the elevated margin ratio only adds to the volatility created by these automated trading algorithms. For all the platforms I use, the execution of margin calls is all automated too. As aforementioned, every issue provides both risks and opportunities simultaneously. Hence, let’s brainstorm some ideas that we can use to either hedge or capitalize on such volatilities. As a disciplined investor, all my specific ideas are fundamentally anchored on a few guiding principles, such as holding various asset classes to decouple from the momentum of the S&P 500, maintaining adequate cash reserves (so you don’t have to join the panic selling yourself if/when it happens), and using a barbell model to delineate risks at a microscopic level.

I put my money where my mouth is and implement these ideas in my accounts. As an example, the next two charts show my survival portfolio’s holdings (the conservative end of my barbell) and also its performance since I started posting it regularly as part of our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) in 2022. As seen, I allocate a good portion to hard currency (GOLD) and maintain a cash reserve in this account. A good portion of this account is also allocated towards low-beta value stocks and funds. These companies are not heavily weighted in passive indices. As a result, these "forgotten" stocks’ performance is less likely to be driven by the actively traded, high-volume index components such as the Mag 7, and consequently, my understanding of their fundamentals plays a larger role in their return/risk ratio. At the same time, I use a growth portfolio as the other extreme of the barbell. This portfolio holds bets that are more aggressive than the SP500 itself and include leveraged ETFs such as TQQQ, small-cap funds such as XBI, and more growth-oriented tactical holdings too.

To conclude, the goal of this article is to draw your attention to other aspects of the SP500 in addition to the P/E ratio. Two particular numbers that provide independent information are the FINRA margin ratio and the fraction of passive trading volume. The current readings for these numbers are more worrisome in my mind than the higher P/E ratios of the S&P 500. Judging by the volatility of the SP500 (either the VIX Index or SPY’s rolling volatility as shown in the last chart), I believe the market is currently underestimating the downward volatility and recommend investors start considering strategies that could either hedge against or capitalize on such a scenario.

data.title: Altria: An Overpriced Melting Ice Cube
data.description:
Over the last 30 years, I have bought and sold Altria (MO) many times. Altria is an incredible company from the point of view of several important financial metrics. And, of course, it has one major flaw: its business is slowly disappearing.

To be clear, I am a fan of investing in MO and have made some nice gains from it in the past. I would not be surprised to buy it again at some point in the future. But the price must be compelling.

In this article I will show some of the company's strengths and then move on to valuation metrics. Finally, I will produce a price at which I would be interested in buying the stock again.

What Makes MO a Great Business

Companies with strong moats usually show great numbers in ROIC (return on invested capital) and margins. These are the first metrics I look at when evaluating any company, and I already know that MO is an absolute home run for these. Let's have a look:

Altria has been consistently in the 30% range for decades, and this is a beast of an ROIC. In fact, most companies with this sort of ROIC would support a P/E of 20+.

Margins show a similar story:

Just for the heck of it, I compared Altria to NVIDIA here. Again, MO shows incredible numbers that would be the envy of any company in any industry.

Another thing that MO does extremely well is it returns profits to shareholders. This is accomplished in three ways: dividends, share reduction, and debt reduction.

We all know that the dividend is great. That's a reason for many to own the stock. As of the writing of this article, the dividend yield is at a very robust 6.3%. There are only a handful of companies with a very safe dividend that sport such a generous yield.

Altria's dedication to share reduction is long-established and exemplary:

Finally, debt reduction has been ongoing, and the company has reduced about 10% since 2020. Here I have to dig them, however, as MO still has a substantial amount of debt on the books thanks to the very ill-advised minority stake purchase of JUUL back in 2018. That wasted $12 billion. So yes, debt is being reduced. A lot of that debt shouldn't be there in the first place.

Overall, Altria is an incredible business in almost every way, from moat to margins to shareholder yield.

The One Little Problem

Altria produces a prodigious amount of cash and is very shareholder friendly with it. Nearly every financial metric for the company is amazing, save one. The business is a "melting ice cube" as several analysts have called it.

Bulls might point to the fact that MO has solid pricing power and has done a good job in ameliorating the declining unit volumes with price increases. That is true up to a point. However, the declining revenue is nonetheless THE problem with the company:

Revenues have been declining and are unlikely to rise substantially in the future. Pricing increases cannot be sustained forever, and profits are going to stagnate at best.

Valuation

Several valuation methods show that MO is at or near the high end of its multiples of recent years. It is clearly expensive based upon its own history. Here are the four horsemen of valuation charts that I like to look at when evaluating a company.

The following are P/E, Price/FCF, EV/EBITDA, and Price/Sales history over the last three years:

Any one of these charts should be cautionary for an investor. Taken together, these are four large red flags waving.

Based on the above, I am looking for the forward P/E to be around 10 before even thinking about buying MO again. That equates to a stock price of around 56.

Discounted Cash Flow Calculation

For the DCF calculation, I'll use a discount rate of 10%. I'll run three calculations: a pessimistic one, a base case (roughly matching analyst estimates), and an optimistic scenario.

Growth stage (5 years) Terminal Stage (15 years) Fair Value
3% -1% 50.0
4% 1% 55.8
7% 3% 68.1

For the optimistic scenario, I purposefully ran numbers that would result in a fair value that is close to where the stock currently trades. In other words, the market is pricing in growth of 7% for 5 years and 3% thereafter (or something similar).

I find the current valuation assumptions wildly optimistic. Based on the DCF, I am looking for MO to fall to about 53 before considering it to be a possible buy again.

Where I Could Be Wrong

Smokeless products. This is the segment of the future for the company. This is where growth can still occur. For the past several years, MO has attempted - and failed miserably - to get smokeless products to be a substantial portion of their revenues.

If Altria cannot just replace lost cigarette revenue with new products, but become a growth company again thanks to new products, I will see MO (and value it) in a whole new light.

Conclusion

Altria is an unusually predictable company, and it is unlikely to produce any major surprises. Looking back at Seeking Alpha's earnings history page shows that the last time MO had an annual earnings surprise of over 0.5% was 2017. The last time revenues surprised by more than 0.5% was 2012!

Similarly, the revisions of the consensus EPS have remained remarkably flat.

Thus, we can be quite confident in what the next few years will bring for Altria. And because of that predictability, the company is rather easy to place a value on. As I wrote above, based on forward P/E, I will start looking at MO again when it falls to 56. Based on the DCF calculation, I will be interested around 53.

As I like to have a margin of safety of at least 10-20%, I'll probably buy shares again if the stock retreats back to the high 40s.

data.title: The Russell 2000 Earnings Leaders Index: Applying A Quality Lens To Small Caps
data.description:
By Daniel Belanger, Quantitative and Fundamental Researcher

Small-cap equities have long played a central role in portfolios seeking growth potential and diversification. For small-cap investors looking to emphasize profitability and operational strength, FTSE Russell has developed the Russell 2000® Earnings Leaders index.

The index applies transparent, rules-based criteria to identify companies within the Russell 2000 universe that demonstrate positive earnings and sustained improvement in key profitability metrics. The Russell 2000 index, launched in 1984, is the original benchmark for US small-cap stocks, providing a consistent and comprehensive view of the performance of the small company segment.

In this FTSE Russell Insight, we discuss why current market conditions have sharpened investor interest in earnings-focused approaches, outline the methodology behind the Russell 2000 Earnings Leaders Index and review the index’s back-tested characteristics.

Seeking quality in a changing market

Recent market conditions have reinforced investor attention on earnings durability, balance sheet strength and operational efficiency. Persistent inflation has continued to pressure profit margins, while interest rates remain structurally higher than in the prior decade, increasing the cost of capital and elevating the importance of cash flow generation. At the same time, we may be in the later stages of the US credit cycle, highlighting the role of financial resilience amid more selective funding environments.

These dynamics have led some small-cap investors to seek systematic approaches that emphasize profitability and improving fundamentals.

A rules-based approach to identifying earnings leadership

The Russell 2000 Earnings Leaders Index applies a transparent, rules-based methodology designed to identify companies within the Russell 2000 universe that exhibit positive earnings and sustained improvement in key profitability measures (Figure 1).

Constituent eligibility is assessed using clearly defined financial metrics derived from company-reported data. These include positive earnings or funds from operations, depending on industry, alongside multi-year improvements in measures such as return on equity and net profit margins, where applicable. To support durability, the methodology requires positive starting values and evaluates improvement over a three-year lookback period, helping to reduce sensitivity to short-term fluctuations.

The index is market-capitalization weighted, with index-level constraints applied to support diversification and regulatory requirements while maintaining representation of the selected company characteristics.

Figure 1. Russell 2000 Earnings Leaders Index Construction Methodology

Screening Methodology
Screen Type Threshold Applicable Industries Additional Notes
Return on Equity Difference from prior 3 years value must be >= 1% All Starting Value (3 years prior) must be greater than zero. Metric is comprised of last 12 months of data.
Net Profit Margin Difference from prior 3 years value must be >= 1% Excludes Financials, Real Estate Starting Value (3 years prior) must be greater than zero. Metric is comprised of last 12 months of data.
Earnings Values Last 12 months of earnings must be greater than zero. Financials Not applicable
Funds from Operations Values (FFO) Last 12 months of FFO must be greater than zero. Real Estate Not applicable

Note: If FFO is not available for a Real Estate firm, we then look at its Earning Value to determine inclusion in the index.

Russell 2000 Earnings Leaders Index characteristics

Over the back-tested period of January 2008 to September 2025, the Russell 2000 Earnings Leaders Index exhibited a distinct mix of return and risk characteristics relative to the Russell 2000 Index (Figure 2).

The Russell 2000 Earnings Leaders Index delivered differentiated performance across market cycles, with periods of meaningful upside participation alongside episodes of lower drawdown sensitivity. These patterns reflect how an emphasis on profitability and balance-sheet metrics has historically interacted with changing market environments, particularly during higher-rate regimes.

Figure 2. Russell 2000 Earnings Leaders Index Risk and Return Statistics, January 2008 – September 2025

Relative performance

Attribution analysis suggests that these outcomes have been driven primarily by company-level selection (+1.75%) rather than sector allocation (+0.37%). The methodology has tended to identify companies with comparatively stronger measured fundamentals within industries, with positive selection effects observed across multiple industries, including Consumer Discretionary, Industrials, Health Care and Technology.

From a factor perspective, back-tested data illustrate that the Russell 2000 Earnings Leaders Index has shown greater alignment with characteristics that are associated with profitability, cash generation and operational efficiency, while momentum exposure has remained broadly comparable to the Russell 2000 Index (Figure 3).

Industry weights further reflect the application of the earnings and improvement criteria (Figure 4). A complementary perspective on small caps

The Russell 2000 Earnings Leaders Index illustrates how a targeted, rules-based lens can be applied within established benchmark frameworks to examine specific company characteristics. data.title: Enbridge: I'm Buying Following This Quarter
data.description:
Introduction

I’ve been a longtime bull on Enbridge (ENB) as I believe that it is one of the companies operating in the midstream sector that has one of the best moats. Since I last reviewed it, ENB has been on a tear, returning 11.19% in stock appreciation in a little bit over 2 months. With the company just releasing earnings and showcasing that it is reinforcing its position as a North American energy infrastructure leader, I believe that this is the right time to showcase why Enbridge is a buy.

Current Dynamics

Q4 has been a landmark period for Enbridge as it showcased significant earnings growth and operational excellence. The company reported GAAP EPS of C$0.89 a share, which is a stark contrast Y/Y, as it was standing at C$0.23 per share. The GAAP figures were boosted by non-cash, unrealized changes in the fair value of derivative instruments; the underlying adjusted metrics tell a story of sustained strength. Adjusted earnings grew by 17% on a Y/Y basis, and estimates were standing at around 77 cents a share, while it came in at C$0.88, which is highly impressive. The beat was largely thanks to higher asset utilization across the liquids and gas transmission networks, as well as the immediate accretive impact of the acquired gas utility business.

Distributable cash flow rose to C$3.21B, up from C$3.07B Y/Y, and supports the company’s goal of maintaining a dividend payout ratio of between 60% and 70% of DCF. Thus, the company increased the quarterly dividend by 3% to reach $3.88 annualized, signifying management’s confidence in the visibility of future cash flows. Plus, this marks the 31st consecutive year of dividend growth.

Diving deeper into ENB’s business segment, the liquid pipelines continue to be the cornerstone of the portfolio, as it generates high cash flows through the irreplaceable Mainline System and associated feeder networks. Adj. EBITDA increased by C$51MM in the quarter compared to the same period in 2024. This was the result of higher demand on the Mainline System and the implementation of annual escalators and surcharges. The Mainline averaged a throughput of 3.1MMbpd through 2025 which is a testament to the system’s strategic importance, plus it was in apportionment for 9 months of the year, telling us that shipper demand consistently exceeded available capacity. This has persisted despite the start of the Trans Mountain Expansion, which management had already factored into long-term financial projections.

The gas transmission segment has also emerged as a primary growth engine, particularly as the demand for natural gas to power data centers and LNG export facilities accelerates. The all of the above approach is perhaps most visible here, where natural gas is positioned as the essential baseload partner for renewable energy. ENB is advancing over 50 data center opportunities across NA, which would require as much as 10 Bcf/d of new takeaway capacity.

On the gas distribution and storage front, since the acquisition of three premier US natural gas utilities from Dominion Energy (D), the utility weight has increased significantly in ENB’s earnings. The segment now serves over 7.1MM customers and is the largest natural gas utility franchise in North America. A critical point of my bullish stance on Enbridge has been the utility segment and its positive regulatory outcomes. See, for example, Enbridge Gas North Carolina reached an interim settlement with an allowed ROE of 9.65% and a $34MM revenue requirement increase. In Utah, a new revenue requirement increase of $62MM became effective at the start of the year. These settlements support quick-cycle capital recovery mechanisms, allowing ENB to reinvest in the safety and reliability of its distribution networks with a predictable return.

Based on this, the company reaffirmed its 2023 to 2026 near-term growth of 7 to 9% for Adj. EBITDA and 4 to 6% for adj. EPS and approximately 3% for DCF per share. Furthermore, the company is expected to see growth accelerate past 2026 to around 5% annually.

Valuation

Enbridge has generally been more expensive than its peers thanks to its Mainline moat and its entrenched position in the Canadian energy landscape. It currently has a P/E Non-GAAP of 25.07, the second highest in the peer group, below Williams (WMB) at 33.87. ENB also has an EV/EBITDA of 16.01, which is at the higher end of the range. And though it has a high FCF generation, the company only has an FCF yield of 2.1% due to the heightened share price. ENB still has a great yield at these prices, as it currently stands at 5.2% on a USD basis.

Looking at the growth prospects, ENB currently has a record-high secured capital backlog of $39B that is diversified across all four business segments, providing a clear runway for growth through the end of the decade. The "all of the above" strategy ensures that ENB is not betting on a single energy outcome but is instead positioned to benefit from the general increase in energy demand, independent of the source. Furthermore, the company has a high degree of protection against inflationary pressures, as over 80% of the company’s EBITDA has built-in inflation protection, either through contractual escalators in its LT agreements or through regulatory mechanisms in its utility business. Plus, the company has a great commercial structure that is incredibly robust, with over 98% of EBITDA derived from regulated or take-or-pay frameworks.

I’m also enthusiastic about the prospects of the company in renewable energy, as it has over 750 MW of renewable power generation under construction, specifically to support Meta’s data center operations. Other projects in the pipeline include the Sequoia solar farm and the Clear Fork Solar project.

Risks

Enbridge still has a significant debt burden, with a current debt-to-EBITDA ratio reported at 4.8x, which is currently at the top of ENB's target range. This means that the company is particularly sensitive to interest rate fluctuations, as higher rates increase financing costs and can pressure DCF down the line

Furthermore, as the company has become so massive, it may become increasingly hard to maintain high per-share growth rates over the long term. As the valuation metrics are higher than peers' average, this could lead to share volatility if growth targets are not met.

Conclusion

Following the quarterly release, I can say that Enbridge is at the height of its operational powers. All of the mentioned tailwinds for shareholders point to the management successfully executing on a long-term strategic vision. I believe that ENB is well-positioned to continue its multi-decade track record of creating shareholder value. I upgrade my rating to a Strong Buy.

data.title: Private Real Estate Debt: A Banner 2025, With More Room To Run
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

Introduction

Total loan originations increased 47% year-to-date through 3Q 2025, reaching their fifth-highest level since 1Q 2002. Outstanding mortgage debt rose to nearly $4.9 trillion as of 2Q 2025, up 4.5% year-over-year. Despite the increase in activity, lending standards remained conservative and did not loosen over the past year. Against this backdrop, the NCREIF/CREFC Open-End Debt Fund Aggregate delivered a 6.1% net*1 total return over the trailing four quarters, but some private real estate debt strategies may have outperformed even more. By comparison, the NCREIF ODCE index generated net total returns of 2.9%*2 and the U.S.-listed REIT index delivered 2.3% gross.*3

Exhibit 1: Annualized return, volatility, and Sharpe ratio, 2014-2025

In our 2026 Inside Real Estate Outlook, we reiterated that commercial real estate (CRE) debt remains our top recommendation across all four quadrants (public vs. private and equity vs. debt). Although increased competition has compressed spreads, we continue to see ample opportunity to originate attractive loans in the year ahead—the Mortgage Bankers Association forecasts total CRE originations to rise +27% in 2026. Risk-adjusted returns remain compelling, supported by conservative loan-to-value ratios on property values that have already reset by roughly 20%.

We also believe the stability of CRE debt funds is underappreciated. As a result, they play an important role in portfolio construction: over the medium to longer term, allocating to CRE debt within a broader mix of private corporate credit or private equity may help to reduce volatility, improve Sharpe ratios, and mitigate drawdown risk. It's possible that these benefits could become even more compelling in the years to come as CRE debt has already experienced a downturn, whereas private corporate credit and private equity have not.

What’s driving the wave of loan originations?

According to the Mortgage Bankers Association (MBA), total CRE mortgage originations increased +27% in 2025 to $634bn. The positive momentum is expected to continue in the year ahead, with originations rising another +27% to $805bn, with core CRE rising +34% and multifamily increasing +21%. Investors often ask why CRE debt markets are gaining momentum even as distress continues to rise. The answer lies in where we are in the cycle and how opportunity is shifting within the market.

  1. We believe the CRE cycle has entered recovery by most traditional measures. Public REIT valuations—often a leading indicator at cyclical turning points—have rebounded meaningfully from their 2023 trough. Private market valuations across major property types have stabilized, and credit markets are once again functioning, supporting renewed transaction activity. Distress, by contrast, is a lagging indicator and continues to rise as the long tail of the downturn works through the system. While elevated delinquencies may weigh on legacy portfolios still absorbing write-downs, they are far less relevant for new funds deploying fresh capital. In this sense, CRE debt has effectively become a “1.0 versus 2.0” market, with new vintages standing to benefit.
  2. The widely discussed wall of maturities represents not just a risk, but a significant opportunity. A large share of loans coming due are refinancing rather than defaulting. Notably, 73% of CMBS conduit loans originally scheduled to mature in 2025 paid off at or before maturity as of the end of 2025, only modestly below the 78% historical average from 2012 to 2024. With approximately $2.1 billion of loans maturing over the next three years, refinancing demand should remain a durable source of origination opportunity.
  3. Transaction activity is recovering. Transaction volumes were 17% higher in the first nine months of 2025 compared with the same period in 2024. Historically, rising volumes have been a reliable signal of improving market confidence, and higher transaction activity directly translates into increased demand for debt capital.
  4. The elevated level of properties delivered in recent years continues to drive financing needs. Newly constructed assets often require bridge loans or permanent take-out financing, adding another layer of demand for CRE debt. While construction activity is expected to moderate going forward, the combination of recent deliveries, loan maturities, and improving transaction volumes supports a robust pipeline of opportunities for lenders.

CRE debt funds have been a clear beneficiary of these dynamics, with their share of total lending rising to a record 14% in 1H25, well above the post-2008 average of roughly 9%. We believe the true share is likely even higher once accounting for the portion of CMBS issuance tied to CRE CLOs, which many CRE debt funds use as financing vehicles. CRE CLO issuance reached its second-highest level since the post-GFC period in 2025, totaling more than $30.5 billion. While competition is increasing, we expect CRE debt funds to remain an integral and durable component of the commercial real estate debt markets.

Exhibit 2: Loan maturities

Attractive risk- and volatility-adjusted returns

Lending standards became less restrictive in 2025 (i.e., less tight), but they did not loosen. This distinction matters. More conservative loan-to-value ratios on property prices that have already reset by roughly 20% help mitigate the risk of further declines in unlevered property valuations. To put the magnitude in perspective, a further reset of this scale would imply cumulative valuation declines of more than 45% this cycle—well beyond the 30%–35% declines experienced in the early 1990s following the Savings & Loans crisis and during the Global Financial Crisis.

Exhibit 3: Sensitivity analysis of hypothetical loan impairment across LTV ranges and CRE price declines

It is also important to remember that CRE mortgages are secured by income-producing real estate—the real estate itself is the collateral, and it does not disappear overnight. In the event of foreclosure, lenders can take ownership of the underlying property at an attractive basis, creating the potential for redevelopment or re-leasing at compelling returns.

We believe this dynamic is a key reason why open-ended CRE debt funds have delivered essentially flat annualized appreciation returns of -0.14% since 2014. The only years with negative returns were -3.1% in 2022, -4.3% in 2023, and -3.9% in 2024, as well as -1.6% in 2020 during COVID. Given our view that the commercial real estate cycle has entered a recovery phase, with prices across major property types having stabilized, we expect appreciation returns for open-ended debt funds to turn positive at some point in 2026.

We believe an even more important consideration is the relative attractiveness of volatility-adjusted returns for CRE debt versus private corporate credit and private equity. The latter two strategies have attracted substantial investor attention over the past decade, largely due to their strong absolute return profiles.

For example, as of 2Q 2025, our analysis of pooled net total returns from Preqin (after management fees and carried interest) shows that North American private equity vehicles (excluding venture capital) have generated 14.6% annualized returns since 1Q 2014, while North American private credit vehicles have delivered 8.7% annualized returns over the same period. By comparison, open-ended CRE debt funds have produced 6.2% annualized total returns, net of fees and promotes, over that same timeframe.

However, CRE debt funds also have much lower volatility than both private corporate credit and private equity over longer periods of time. As a result, we think the portfolio optimization benefits of adding an allocation of CRE debt to either alternative strategy is likely underappreciated. In Exhibit 4, we show modern portfolio statistics (MPTs) at various allocation bands based on a back test of returns since 1Q 2014. In every instance, Sharpe ratios are improved and a drawdown risk is mitigated by adding an allocation of open-ended CRE debt funds to a portfolio of private equity or private credit. While investors do give up some returns, we think it's outweighed by the benefits.

Exhibit 4: Analysis of impacts of various allocations of open-ended CRE debt funds to a portfolio of private equity and private credit (2014 – 3Q25 back test)

Private CRE Credit vs. Private Equity (2014 - 3Q25)

Private CRE Credit vs. Private Credit (2014 - 3Q25)

Source: NCREIF/CREFC, Preqin, Principal Real Estate, 3Q 2025. MDD = max drawdown

Conclusion

Commercial real estate debt delivered strong performance in 2025 and is well-positioned for continued success in 2026 and beyond. With loan originations surging, lending standards remaining conservative, and property valuations having stabilized after meaningful resets, we believe CRE debt offers compelling risk-adjusted returns in today's environment.

The fundamentals supporting CRE debt are on solid footing: a substantial refinancing pipeline, recovering transaction activity, and ongoing financing needs from recent property deliveries should sustain strong origination volumes. Meanwhile, conservative loan-to-value ratios on already-reset property values provide meaningful downside protection—a stark contrast to private corporate credit and private equity markets that have yet to experience a downturn this cycle.

Perhaps most importantly, CRE debt's stability and low volatility make it an effective portfolio diversifier. Our analysis demonstrates that allocating to open-ended CRE debt funds alongside private equity or private credit strategies can improve Sharpe ratios and reduce drawdown risk, even if it means modestly lower absolute returns.

As investors increasingly focus on risk management and portfolio resilience, we believe these benefits position CRE debt as a valuable complement to other private market allocations.

With the commercial real estate cycle having entered recovery and property prices rising again, thereby limiting losses, we maintain our conviction that CRE debt represents one of the most attractive opportunities across both public and private real estate markets today. Indeed, the best vintage returns for CRE debt funds historically occur in the early stages of an early cycle environment.

Exhibit 5: Historical range of IRRs by vintage

Footnotes

*1 Source: NCREIF, 3Q 2025

*2 Source: NCREIF, 4Q 2025

*3 Source: Bloomberg, 4Q 2025.
